data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 214: $21,400 Allocated, $2,152.32 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
Liberation Day turned into market destruction day as the announcement of reciprocal tariffs caused the S&P 500 to decline by -7.9% while the Nasdaq fell by -7.94% this week. All the gains from the past year have been erased, and the S&P 500 is approaching bear market territory as it's fallen by 17.46% since making new all-time highs in February. Fed Chair Powell spoke on Friday and stood his ground on rate cuts while indicating that the impact from tariffs could be larger than initially projected. The only change that I recognized was that he indicated that the Fed would need to balance fighting inflation with protecting the labor market. We got the U.S. non-farm payrolls on Friday, and the U.S. economy added 228,000 jobs in March, which was well above the consensus forecast of 135,000. In my opinion, the market is overreacting as there simply isn't enough data to support the rapid descent in equities. Nothing is finalized, and for all we know, countries could be negotiating behind the scenes, and we could get an onslaught of new data over the next several days. I am more concerned about what the CEOs will say during the Q1 earnings calls and if guidance will remain the same for 2025 or be lowered. A lot can happen in the next several weeks, and I just think there isn't enough data to predict accurately how companies will be impacted. If tariffs go into effect without negotiations, then the market could be in for a rough 2025, but there isn't enough information to make that determination, and the selloff is just a knee-jerk reaction at this point. I am buying into weakness, and for the time being, I am not worried about tariffs.

The markets sent every portfolio into a downward spiral, even the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. After allocating $21,400 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished in week 214 at $24,808.46. This was the largest weekly loss as the account's profitability decreased by -$1,953.93 to $3,408.46, which is a return on invested capital of 15.93%. After declining by -9.25%, I continued to sleep like a baby, and I am looking at the recent price action as an opportunity to buy. This week, I added to my positions in BP p.l.c. (BP), and the BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust (BSTZ). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $105.28 of dividend income in week 214. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $23.60 (1.11%) to $2,152.32. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $3,791.52 of income since inception, which is 17.72% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. While this week wasn't fun for many investors, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has an 8.68% yield on the current asset value and a 10.06% yield on cost. I am ready for choppy waters and still have a significant cushion between profitability and allocated capital. I am looking forward to generating recurring income, which will be immediately reinvested while adding to positions I feel are deeply discounted. Regardless if it takes 2 weeks or 1 year for the market to rebound, I will be allocating capital each week and building out the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

This was a brutal week for the investment community, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio wasn't spared. Nothing worked this week, and even positions that have limited tariff exposure, such as SL Green Realty (SLG), fell by -10.75%. Volatility is part of investing, and I am confident in the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's strategy. I wish I would allow myself to invest more than $100 per week at times like this because I see so many opportunities, but I have to stay within the original framework of the portfolio and replicate investing on a set budget. This week, the account's profitability fell by -9.25%, and this was the largest weekly drop on both a dollar and percentage basis. I do believe that the strategy will prove to work well over the coming weeks as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will continue to generate recurring income, which will be reinvested, and I will be adding to positions, no matter what happens. I am still up 15.93% on invested capital and generating a portfolio yield of 8.68%, which will help me ride out of the storm.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $646.77 (30.05%)
  • Equities $520.68 (24.19%)
  • CEFs $385.41 (17.91%)
  • REITs $361.51 (16.80%)
  • BDCs $237.96 (11.06%)

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 14 weeks into 2025, $613.94 of dividend income was produced, which is 37.82% of the total income from 2024. There were 205 positions that paid a dividend, which is 28.91% of the dividends generated in 2024.

March is finished, and a new record has been established, as there was $105.28 of dividend income generated in week 214. This is almost double week 14 in 2024, as $54.04 was generated. This is my first triple-digit week, and it helped drive a lot of numbers higher. My weekly dividend income average in 2025 is $43.85, which is an increase of $12.64 (40.49%) YoY. It's going to be interesting to see if this will be the largest amount of weekly dividend income generated, or if I can get close to the $125 level before 2025 is over. I think there is a lot of potential, and the dividend snowball continues to grow as more income is generated each and every week.

Q1 is officially over, and my new projections are very bullish when it comes to income generation. In Q1 2024, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $321.80. This increased by 66.45% ($213.83) in Q1 2025 to $535.63. March ended up being a very strong month as $189.26 of dividend income was produced. If I assume that I will produce 66.45% more dividend income in 2025 than I did in 2024, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will generate $2,701.59 in dividend income, which is significantly more than my original assumptions. I am very interested to see how Q2 plays out as the dividend income generated gradually increases throughout the year during 2024. While the market crashed this week, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio was generating recurring income, and I am excited about all the future progress that is being made.

The market performance this week has helped some positions get into the green section of the table below, which represents all the positions that are generating at least 1 share from their dividends. There are now 44 positions accomplishing this, and the additional shares are projected to produce an additional $245.82 in dividend income. While falling markets are never fun, I am excited about buying into weakness as I see a lot of deals on the horizon. I am going to do what I can to allocate capital toward positions that are outside of the green section while equities are on sale.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

REITs are still the largest segment in the portfolio, and I believe this will be the story for a while. Individual equities now represent 38.48% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 24.19% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.52% of the portfolio and generate 75.81% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I think there are a lot of opportunities in energy and financials, so don't be surprised if I am adding to positions in these segments over the next several weeks.

The top-10 holdings are starting to look out of sync again, but I think this is going to end up being an anomaly. I was having a conversation with someone about Altria Group (MO) this week, and it was incredible how low shares were last year. I haven't added to MO since February 2025, and it's still 4.85% of the portfolio. I think that over 2025, I will get the top-20 positions to even out further, and I would like to see MO and Realty Income (O) represent less than 4% of the portfolio heading into the end of the year.

The NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI) is no longer part of the top-10 holdings, and for the first time, British American Tobacco (BTI) is making an appearance. This has changed the dynamics of the top 10 a bit, and several positions have paid their dividends this week. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,129.83, and they finished the week with a value of $7,690.36. This is an ROI of $1,560.53 (25.46%). There has been $1,072.79 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 17.50% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $585.81 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.56%. These positions represent 31% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.22% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 214 Additions

In week 214 I added to my positions in BP p.l.c., and the BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust.

BP

  • Energy took a hit this week, and BP fell -15.91%, pushing its dividend yield to 6.61%
  • I think we are going to need more energy across all forms to support the data center build-out that is occurring
  • BP has always traded at a discount to the other supermajors, and I think that the current price is a drastic discount from where it should trade
  • Regardless of what happens with tariffs, I am excited to add to BP under $30 as they are generating $12 billion in free cash flow (FCF), buying back stock, and paying a large dividend

BSTZ

  • I wanted to get BSTZ to generate at least 1 share per year, so I added enough shares this week to make that happen
  • BSTZ has a large allocation to the Magnificent Seven, and with the amount of value that was lost in the market, I wanted to add to a position that had exposure to big tech
  • I think that the market will be higher a year from now than it is today, so getting BSTZ in a deep correction was very exciting to me
  • BSTZ now has a dividend yield of almost 16%, and I am looking forward to all of the income being reinvested

Week 215 Game Plan

Next week, I am probably going to allocate capital toward BP again, Kinder Morgan (KMI), and the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

Conclusion

This was a difficult week in the markets, but the dividend income rolling in certainly softened the blow. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is still in the black and is projected to generate $2,152.32 of dividend income for the remainder of the year if I don't allocate another dollar. Now that Q1 is finished, I have updated my projections, and I believe that I was very conservative with my original estimates, and there is the potential to exceed $2,700 of dividend income by the end of the year. I am not worried about tariffs yet, as I don't have enough information to make an accurate assessment. I want to see how the next several weeks play out and what CEOs are saying on their earnings calls. I am excited to allocate capital into a down market and look forward to seeing how this story plays out. Please leave all of your suggestions below, as I try to respond to everyone in the comment section.

data.title: GreensKeeper Q1 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the GreensKeeper Value Fund Q1 2025 Letter.

Top Contributors

  • The top contributor to the portfolio in Q1 was Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B) with a return of +17.5%. Berkshire finished 2024 on a strong note, with operating earnings rising 27.1% from the prior year. The Insurance and Utilities & Energy segments showed stark improvement, with profits increasing 66% and 60% respectively year-over-year. With over $300 billion of idle cash at year-end, Buffett may finally be getting his opportunity to bag an elephant. Like his major acquisitions of the past, these moves drive the future growth of the company’s cash generation machine.
  • Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) was our second-best performer in the quarter, +20.4%. Growing demand for its Cystic Fibrosis ('CF') franchise continued to generate substantial free cash flow. Vertex remains focused on diversifying its product portfolio and recently launched two new approved therapies: Alyftrek and Journavx. Alyfrtek is an improvement to VRTX’s CF portfolio, offering a once-daily dosing regimen that is far more convenient for patients. Importantly, it covers 31 additional rare mutations of CF for which no previous treatment existed. Journavx, a non-opioid pain signal inhibitor for acute pain, is beginning to ramp up sales as physicians gain confidence in its real-world efficacy and as VRTX advances reimbursement negotiations with payors. We believe that Journavx has the potential to become a blockbuster therapy, providing significant quality-of-life improvements to patients. In addition, VRTX is developing therapies for chronic pain that utilize a similar mechanism of action—blocking selective sodium channels—to inhibit the transmission of pain signals with little off-target effects. Success would be great news for shareholders, but more importantly, offer a powerful alternative to opioids and help combat the opioid crisis.
  • Our third top performer in the quarter was Check Point Software Technologies (CHKP) +22.1%. CHKP’s enterprise cybersecurity solutions continue to gain traction in the market, supported by growing demand for advanced network security. Recently appointed CEO Nadav Zafrir, a pioneer in the Israeli cyber-security market, has bolstered the company’s executive sales force in efforts to accelerate growth. CHKP is well-capitalized, with over $2.5 billion of excess cash on the balance sheet, and continues to actively repurchase its own shares.
  • Rounding out our top 5 performers in Q1 were Elevance Health (ELV) +17.9% and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) + 15.8%. As mentioned in the last Scorecard, we believe the sell-off in ELV over the past year has been overdone, and the stock is trading at a significant discount to our estimate of its intrinsic value. The recent rebound reflects a partial correction of that mispricing. ICE continues to perform well as a significant portion of its earnings is driven by transaction volume on its exchanges and increasing demand for financial data, both of which generally increase when panic-induced volatility hits the markets.

Top Detractors

  • Our biggest laggard in the first quarter was Alphabet Inc. (GOOG, GOOGL) -18.3%. Shares sold off following the company’s year-end earnings release in February, where it announced plans to increase capital expenditures by a substantial $23 billion in 2025. Recently, GOOG also announced its intention to acquire cybersecurity firm Wiz Inc. for $32 billion, aiming to strengthen its position in the cloud computing market. The scale of these investments is substantial, but we note that GOOG generated $116 billion in operating profits during 2024 and ended the year with $93 billion of excess cash on its balance sheet. In our opinion, the increased capital expenditures are necessary to maintain GOOG’s position in its core search market. Beyond search, GOOG continues to build value across YouTube, Google Cloud, Waymo, and DeepMind, all of which leverage GOOG’s enviable infrastructure.
  • Our second-largest contractor in the quarter was American Express (AXP). AXP finished 2024 on a strong note, with earnings growing 23% for the year. While provisions for credit losses increased, reflecting a more cautious credit outlook, net write-off rates remained below historical averages, indicating strength in the underlying credit quality. AXP remains well-capitalized, and its affluent customer base is expected to fare better than the general economy in the coming months. However, the stock was not cheap, and we trimmed our position in Q1.

One thing that is common across both our top performers and contractors is that they are well-capitalized businesses with durable competitive positions in their respective markets. That is by design. Companies that operate from a position of strength are better positioned to navigate market downturns and often emerge in a stronger position as competitors go bankrupt or are acquired. Moreover, many of our portfolio companies actively repurchase their own stock. Lower stock prices allow them to repurchase a greater percentage of their outstanding shares, leaving remaining shareholders with a larger claim on future cashflows. This is a powerful and tax-free mechanism for long-term value creation.

New Purchase

We made one new purchase in the quarter: Icon PLC (ICLR). ICLR is a leading contract research organization ('CRO') based in Ireland that manages clinical trials for pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies worldwide. Despite some uncertainty driven by recent personnel changes at the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), we believe that new drugs and medical devices will continue to be developed, and scientific progress will continue. New drugs require rigorous clinical testing before reaching the market. As a leading CRO, ICLR will capture its fair share of clinical trials once the dust settles.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: What Gold Is Telling Us
data.description:
By Brien Lundin

It isn’t often that you check the markets over your first cup of coffee and find gold up over $100.

But, as you can see from the pic I snapped above, that’s precisely what greeted me as I switched on CNBC this morning. (And yes, I am a closed-captioning boomer.)

Shades of October 2008...and March 2020!

Over the past week, we’ve seen similarities with those two previous crises in that gold and silver have been alternately sold off as investors desperately raised cash for margin calls, and then bouncing higher as traders bet on more mayhem and easier money.

Today (or rather, last night) we got another throwback — the feeling that the global financial system was in danger.

That’s because, contrary to the wishes and the strategy of the Trump administration, Treasury yields spiked overnight.

As our friend (and New Orleans Conference alum) Jim Bianco posted on X this morning:

Something has broken tonight in the bond market. We are seeing a disorderly liquidation.

If I had to GUESS, the basis trade is in full unwind.

Since Friday's close to now ... the 30-year yield is up 56 bps, in three trading days.

The last time this yield rose this much in 3 days (close to close) was January 7, 1982, when the yield was 14%.

This kind of historic move is caused by a forced liquidation, not human managers making decisions about the outlook for rates at midnight ET.

The “basis trade” has been a darling of hedge funds, which bet on the arbitrage between spot Treasury yields and futures, using massive leverage on this tiny spread to get enough torque to make it worthwhile.

It’s the kind of trade that emerges when there are vast oceans of liquidity sloshing around the markets looking for any advantage. And those oceans are the result, as I’ve been writing ad nauseam, of decades of ever-easier monetary policies.

It’s also the kind of trade that can go radically south on an unexpected turn in the markets, as will happen when the rest of the world decides to sell anything associated with the U.S. and its currency.

What Alan Greenspan Warned Me Of...

It reminds me of a conversation I had with Alan Greenspan about a decade ago.

I was sharing my concern over the expanding federal debt, which was at that time just a fraction of today’s level, but still worrisome to me.

Greenspan shared my worries about the federal debt but then confided that what really concerned him was the derivative exposure of the “too big to fail” institutions. Nominally, that exposure is denominated not in trillions, but somewhere above a quintillion.

As Greenspan pointed out to me, even those institutions couldn’t get a handle on their liabilities, since their derivatives were interconnected with those held by other institutions...and all their exposure was now backed by Uncle Sam!

The crack-up of the Treasury basis trade, along with nations and global investors shedding their U.S. exposure to equities, Treasurys, and the dollar, directly impacts that derivative market.

And gold, as you can see below, has been soaring in response.

After trading off of its morning highs for a while, gold is now rocketing higher again. And so are U.S. equities, as the Trump administration has announced a 90-day pause in the reciprocal tariffs...although China is still in the crosshairs thanks to their retaliatory responses.

The rally in gold today, as well as over the past year or so, has been nothing short of remarkable. As you know, now and then over this new bull market, I’ve rhetorically pondered, “What is gold telling us?”

Is it seeing stagflation ahead, with the trade war bringing a recession along with higher inflation? Yes.

Is it the Fed’s debt trap, with over $9 trillion of Treasuries that need to be refinanced this year at much higher interest rates? Yes.

Is it the teetering bond market and the risk of contagion endangering the global financial system? Yes.

Is it a global flight from dollar hegemony, not only central banks but investors across the globe? Yes.

In short, gold has been telling us that it’s not one thing. It’s everything.

After decades of ever-easier money, ever-greater financialization and globalization, ever-larger debts, and more, gold remains the only thing that protects against everything.

The good news is that not only are gold and silver soaring, but investors are finally jumping onto the mining stocks to gain exposure to the already high metals prices.

Original Post

data.title: GOVT: Put Defense On The Field, Tariffs Are Only A Piece Of The Puzzle
data.description:
Today's piece focuses on the iShares U.S. Treasury Bond ETF (BATS:GOVT), a U.S. treasury bond vehicle with hybrid maturity exposure. And no: This isn't another article solely based on tariffs. Sure, tariffs are discussed in the report. However, instead of isolating tariffs out of panic, we decided to form a holistic view of GOVT for investors seeking active and passive returns.

Without delay, herewith are our thoughts of GOVT's prospects.

Fund Overview

GOVT invests throughout the treasury yield curve. The fund currently possesses a 4.14% SEC yield, convexity of 0.71, and effective duration of 5.73 years. In essence, its current exposure isolates short-to-intermediate term treasury bonds with positive convexity and a hedge against credit spread risk.

An expanded view of the ETF's exposure affirms its commitment to investing throughout the curve. It maintains conviction to numerous maturities, meaning it doesn't follow a laddered approach. Nonetheless, throughout-the-curve exposure is visible as opposed to pure key rate conviction.

Kindly note the following regarding the above.

  1. Exposure may change as time passes.
  2. Isolating a key rate refers to isolating points on the yield curve instead of the entire curve.

Time series data illustrates that GOVT's investors have mainly relied on distributions since the vehicle's inception. However, as explained later in the article, capital gains can play a significant role during volatile market environments, which is echoed by the vehicle's occasional decoupling from investment grade bonds.

Our View Of The Market (Not Just Tariffs)

Trump Eases Tariffs

As most might be aware, the financial markets found respite after President Trump suspended the U.S.'s reciprocal tariff program by 90 days (ex-China). The Nasdaq subsequently experienced its second-best day in history and certain bond ETFs rallied, highlighting investors' sensitivity to the global trade war.

Aside: Here's a detailed explanation of the tariff debacle - link here.

Do we think everything is settled and back to normal after the announcement? No, definitely not. In fact, we've argued for weeks that the U.S. economy is in late cycle, meaning its cyclical growth outlook is diminishing. In our view, the reciprocal tariff announcement merely acted as an accelerator to the diminishing growth outlook and raised the possibility of a hard landing. Moreover, we think events such as the reciprocal tariff announcement will be frequent during President Trump's second tenure. What do I mean by that? In simple terms, the tariff announcement will likely be one of a series of volatile policy shift events that might occur under Trump 2.0; we don't see the trade war leaving the building anytime soon, nor do we see attempts at fiscal reforms diminishing.

Our market outlook is based on a gradual decay of business and consumer sentiment instead of isolating tariffs. That said, the tariff debacle provided valuable inferences to those seeking market sentiment indicators; let's revise what occurred in the past week while considering historical precedent to see how bonds could play out in the near future.

Re-emphasizing The Market's Behavior During The Tariff Debacle

I want to start this subsection by highlighting BCA Research's Peter Berezin's recent commentary, where he delivered an opinion about bond yields in the aftermath of reciprocal and retaliation tariff announcements. According to Berezin, “One thing that I find rather disconcerting now is that bond yields haven't fallen,” he added: "Usually, bond yields fall during periods of growth worries, and that helps to support the economy. We haven't seen that this time around."

Berezin's commentary carries gravity. However, many have forgotten that bond yields dropped in the two trading sessions succeeding Trump's reciprocal tariff announcements and that yields only spiked when retaliation occurred from adversaries. I'm not saying that last week's behavior outweighs this week's; I'm merely highlighting the possibility that market participants are overly focused on intra-day activity instead of repricing their portfolios according to longer-term expectations, and therefore overreaction has surfaced. In our view, Aswath Damodaran provided a more measured approach to today's market environment (not just bonds), when he recently published an article stating that he currently doesn't "see much gain watching the market hour-to-hour and day-to-day, since its doings are out of my control and anything that I do in response is more likely to do harm than good. Instead, I plan on living my life, enjoying life's small pleasures."

Having said all that, let's traverse into a statistic analysis to quantify our own outlook.

Where do we price yields?

The following diagram shows where yields were placed before the U.S. market opened on Wednesday. Having initially dropped since Trump's announcement, yields skyrocketed between Monday morning and Wednesday afternoon. Although debatable, we think the market developed a dual-scenario, being 1) overreacting to the potential of a short-term inflation spike, and/or 2) pricing a stagflation scenario. We disagreed with the outlook at the time, instead leaning toward a hardish landing scenario, prompting us to place more conviction in treasuries. However, as noted before, Trump slammed the brakes on his tariff plan, which altered the market's demeanor.

The next chart shows the yield curve's position during April 10, pre-market. Yields settled lower across maturities, indicating that investors have priced a lower inflation outlook. Despite withdrawing his initial plan, Trump's protectionist outlook remains, with a 10% standardized tariff and a hardline restriction on China being intact. Thus, the suspension of the reciprocal tariff program doesn't mean abolishment of the overall agenda. Therefore, we think investors have overreacted once again, especially if their base case is higher tariffs = sustainable consumer demand = higher inflation.

Our own curve positioning outlook is summarized in a building block approach, including the real rate, expected inflation, the term premium, and a liquidity premium, allowing a more holistic analysis of matters.

Real Rate & Expected Inflation

In our opinion, real rates will stay level in the short term, possibly reaching 1.90% with a fade closer to the 1% zone across the yield curve. We see inflation increasing slightly within the next six months due to the 10% standardized tariff and ongoing trade war with China (currently tariffed at 125%). However, we think higher prices will diminish consumer demand. In fact, we've already seen signs of a diminishing demand outlook after U.S. consumer sentiment for March decreased to 57 points, the lowest since February 2022. In conjunction, we see business capital expenditures dropping in due course, mostly due to global uncertainty (another tariff event or equivalent could break loose at anytime) but also due to the possibility of the U.S. being in late cycle growth.

In line with lower real rates, we expect inflation to drop after an initial six-month spike, whereby an increase to 3%+ within six months could be matched with sub-2% inflation rates throughout the curve. Remember, inflation isn't merely about supply-side factors; demand is elastic, and although sticky at times, plays a key role in establishing price levels.

Term Premium

For those unaware, the term premium describers a risk premium demanded by the market for holding longer-term securities. In our experience, the premium is mostly impacted by inflation uncertainty and fiscal macroeconomic doubts, yet supply/demand factors often dictate the premium.

We expect a high term premium or short-term bonds (measured versus cash positions). However, given our doubtful macroeconomic outlook, we think the term premium will turn negative for bonds ranging between -0.2% and 0.5% for 5-, 10-, and 15-year treasuries due to investors' rising demand for flight-to-safety assets. Sure, market behavior in the past week counters our argument; however, we think the market failed to price a hard landing and instead opted for a stagflation scenario paired with a run to cash. In contrast to the negative term premiums forecast for 5-, 10-, and 15-year bonds, we think the longer end will post a positive term premium, mostly due to repricing of an eventual recovery - keep in mind, structural economic reforms such as a 4th industrial revolution might still bake into a long-term real growth recovery.

Supply demand factors have worked against our outlook in recent quarters, with the New York Fed's open market purchases receding and non-government investors' demand being flat. However, signs of a pivot have neared. For instance, the 10-year note auction drew strong demand on Wednesday, selling $39 billion at a yield of 4.435%, below the estimate of 4.465%.

Illiquidity Premium

The illiquidity premium is similar to the term premium but isolates investors' demand for holding an asset purely based on liquidity. Similar to the term premium, the illiquidity premium can be influenced by factors such as asymmetrical demand. For example, newly issued treasuries may receive excess demand for off-the-run treasuries.

In this instance, we decided to look at the G-Spread, which parts yields of on-the-run versus off-the-run government bonds. As shown in the diagram, the G-Spread is usually around sub-4 basis points but can escalate to 10+. In our base case, we estimated a G-Spread of 0.02% for six month notes, 0.05% for 5-years, 0.10% for 10-year bonds, and 0.15% for 15-years. In contrast, we expect reversion to 0.10% for 25-year assets.

Bid-ask spreads remain tame for GOVT. However, we expect market depth and width to divert towards a rocky road scenario, in turn, spiking the G-spread and illiquidity premium alike.

Adding It All Together

As previously mentioned, we think yields have baked a stagflation scenario, which we disagree with. The curve's current par rates illustrate the market's base case. Therefore, we didn't plot estimated yields in that regard. Having said that, we plotted yields related to our outlook, which is either of 1) A moderate recession and 2) A hard landing.

Aside: Before reading the numbers, note that this is our estimate and that it's point-in-time. Market participants' views might differ and the curve changes throughout time.

Herewith are our yield estimates for a moderate recession. GOVT current possesses an effective duration of 5.73 years, meaning its emphasis is on the 5-year yield. Though, as mentioned, the vehicle's key rate conviction can change throughout time. We see GOVT benefitting from an overall drop in yields and believe longer-term or a hybrid approach could result in excess returns within the next 12-to-18 months.

Premium/Term 6-M 5-Y 10-Y 15-Y 25-Y
Real Rate 1.90% 1.30% 1.20% 1.10% 1.20%
Inflation Estimate 3.00% 1.80% 1.75% 1.85% 1.95%
Term Premium 0.02% -0.5% -0.2% -0.5% 0.05%
Liquidity Premium 0.02% 0.05% 0.10% 0.15% 0.10%
Total 4.94% 3.10% 3.03% 3.05% 3.20%

Source: Author's Work - A Moderate Recession Scenario, Non-Crisis, No-Stagflation

Here's our hard landing scenario. This would require a deep regression in real growth, yet, it remains a possibility, especially if global macro turmoil persists.

Premium/Term 6-M 5-Y 10-Y 15-Y 25-Y

Source: Author's Work - A Hardish Landing Scenario, Non-Crisis, No-Stagflation

I close this section with a time series chart of the on-the-run 10-year yield, allowing readers to make up their own minds about yield trajectories according to their predicted scenarios.

Why A Hybrid Approach Could Work

The yield curve has levelled up and down quite abruptly year-to-date, especially during the tariff debacle. However, we expect a volatile economic environment to trigger non-parallel movements due to partitioned supply-demand at various stages of the curve. Moreover, volatile incremental differences in economic outlook may result in kinks within the curve. Whether iShares' GOVT will capitalize on such situations remains to be seen. However, unlike IEF ETF (IEF), ZROZ ETF (ZROZ), and TLT ETF (TLT), GOVT doesn't isolate a certain maturity bucket, meaning it's a de-risked vehicle, which still retains a minor yield enhancement methodology.

Furthermore, a hybrid approach allows more constant maturity than a conviction vehicle, allowing a smoothed approach to monthly distributions, reducing investors' re-investment risk.

We at Pearl Gray Equity and Research are committed to the longer end of the curve, yet we acknowledge that some investors would prefer less conviction, making GOVT a prime candidate.

GOVT's Other Key Metrics

To reiterate GOVT's characteristics, it has a 30-day SEC yield of 4.14%, which is quite impressive, considering a vehicle such as ZROZ ETF has a 30-day yield of merely 46 basis points higher (4.60%). Paired with that, GOVT has a standard deviation of 0.30% versus ZROZ's 1.46%. While not a valid pure relative comparison, the juxtaposition conveys GOVT's solid risk-return dynamics. Further, the vehicle has positive convexity and isolates against credit risk: two factors we deem highly favorable in the current market environment.

Risks

Collateralized Bonds Often Sell Cheap During Market Turmoil

The reality is that bonds sold off since the start of the week, counteracting the belief that treasuries usually act as a risk-off haven. Rumors such as China purposely executing U.S. bond sales and basis trade behavior certainly hold validity. However, we think a more important matter to outline is the fact that bonds can sell at discounts during market turmoil as they act as collateral to many investment funds, dealers, and other market participants. Thus, although bonds can isolate against a recession, technical factors might work against the dynamic.

Or... The Market Could Just Go Risk-On Again

On the other end of the spectrum, we could see a resurgence in risk-on assets, which have oversold in the past week. Intertwined with a stronger-than anticipated economy, our base case falls flat. As such, we acknowledge the possibility of our high conviction outlook being overzealous.

Conclusion

While the market focused on tariffs and decoupling of bond-to-recession behavior, we formulated a deeper view by considering tariffs and decoupling as pieces of a broader puzzle. It's in our belief that the U.S. is in late cycle growth and that isolated events such as reciprocal tariff announcements merely accelerate the process.

We expected a semi hard landing to occur, deeming it an excellent time to commit to a bond vehicle such as GOVT ETF, which possesses compelling yields and low standard deviation, achieved by investing throughout the yield curve via a hybrid approach. In addition, some might disagree, but we see 10-year yields falling to about 3% in the next 12-months, providing GOVT with substantial systematic support.

Consensus: Very Bullish/'Strong Buy'

data.title: GE HealthCare: Looking Healthier Here
data.description:
Shares of GE HealthCare (NASDAQ:GEHC) have been a publicly traded entity since early 2023, when the healthcare business was spun off from GE. A $60 stock at the time, has largely traded in a $60-$90 range in the more than two years which followed, as shares now trade at $65 per share.

This makes that investors have not seen any real capital gains since the separation from its former parent company while the business has delivered on sales and margin improvements, the combination of which creates a more compelling picture here, even as the outlook for 2025 is of course highly uncertain now.

A World Where Healthcare Has No Limits

The paragraph header is the mission of GE HealthCare, being a major global healthcare provider. The business generates nearly $20 billion in sales and reports its results across four segments.

The largest of these is an $8.9 billion imaging business, which includes a range of scanning and imaging devices. While this is the biggest segment of GE, it is among the lower margin activities, with segment margins reported around the 10% mark.

The second-largest division is the advanced visualizations solutions business, a $5.2 billion business. Solutions include visualization tools for 2D and 3D images during surgery, as these are very lucrative activities, with margins reported in the low-twenties.

Patient Care Solutions make up about $3.1 billion in sales, with these activities including ECG solutions for more precise diagnostics and treatment plans, with margins reported in the low-teens. The smallest Pharmaceutical Diagnostics business generated $2.5 billion in sales, yet on a relative basis is the most lucrative segment, which reports margins in the mid-twenties.

The whole idea behind the spin-off and separation which commenced two years ago is to make GE HealthCare is better focused business, with room for own capital allocation decisions. The business posted standalone adjusted EBIT margins around 14.5% at the time of the spin-off, based on 2022 results, as these margins have advanced to 16.3% in 2024.

Focus on improved growth, innovation, productivity gains, general & administrative optimizations, all should result in segment margins in the high-teens, perhaps topping 20% by 2026-2028. In order to achieve this, GE HealthCare has upped R&D spending since the spin-off, as well as embarked on some deals.

Reading Into The Expectations

Early in 2024, the company reported its 2023 results, basically the first real year in which GE HealthCare operated as a standalone business. Revenues rose by a solid 8% to $19.6 billion, with almost all growth achieved on an organic basis.

The P&L line was a bit harder to read into, this being the first year in which GE Healthcare reported as a standalone entity. GAAP earnings were reported at $1.4 billion, equal to $3.03 per share. Adjusted for quite a few items like preferred stock dividends, restructuring costs, acquisition related charges, spinoff cost and amortization costs, adjusted earnings were reported down $0.70 per share to $3.93 per share. The decline in earnings can largely be attributed to incurred interest costs, now operating on a standalone basis.

The company guided for 2024 sales to be up around 4%, a 50-80 basis point adjusted EBIT margin improvements, all to result in adjusted earnings moving up to $4.20-$4.35 per share.

2024 - A Mixed Bag

As it turned out, as reported early in 2025, GE HealthCare has seen 2024 sales rise by just a percent to $19.7 billion, in part the result of adverse currency moves. That, however, was offset by an 80 basis point improvement in EBIT margins, allowing the company to post adjusted earnings at $4.49 per share, ahead of the original guidance, even as sales came in soft.

Net debt was pretty modest at $6.0 billion as reported EBITDA came in at around $2.9 billion, that is before adjustments, for a leverage ratio sub 2 times, that is even as the company spent over $300 million on M&A efforts in the year.

While topline sales growth was a bit underwhelming over the past year, the company reported a 6% increase in the order intake, with the book-to-bill ratio reported at 1.09 times. Despite this order intake, the company guided for a relatively modest 2-3% increase in 2025 sales, with subdued growth in demand attributed to tough conditions in China.

Adjusted EBIT margins are seen up another 40-50 basis points to 16.7-16.8% of sales. This makes that the company guided for a 3-6% increase in adjusted earnings to $4.61-$4.75 per share, with free cash flow seen in excess of $1.75 billion, equal to $3.80 per share.

What Now?

After shares peaked at levels in the low-nineties in February, shares have moved lower to $65 amidst the turmoil in the wider markets. These headwinds have weighed on the stock, as it can be argued that the business might be a bit less substantial to headwinds given the nature of the activities and the location thereof.

Based on the forward earnings guidance, a 20 times forward earnings guidance has been cut to 14 times, dramatically improving appeal, and tolerating lower earnings power (for a while) as well. All this makes that appeal is on the increase if you ask me, as GE HealthCare is making steady inroads over time.

The current improvements look a bit soft compared to the 2026–2028 targets, yet the direction of travel is in the right direction, amidst a reasonable balance sheet, and now appealing valuation has well. At some point in time, the business has to deliver on this, amidst the absence of share buybacks and a mere 0.2% dividend yield, which makes that investors have to resort to growth and rising share prices to see any returns here.

Right now, I see a headwind to the earnings guidance, driven by the intensified tariffs, which now seem to center and focus on China. That and economics slowdown is a clear negative; however, the recent dollar weakness actually might provide a boost to earnings here. This may provide a dampening effect on the bottom line.

Given all this, shares look cheap as the business has seen improvements since the separation, while shares now traded stagnant as well, the combination of which makes me cautiously upbeat on GE HealthCare, as continued progress and re-rating should drive investor returns from here.

data.title: Google Sets The Stage For Hybrid AI With Cloud Next Announcements
data.description:
There’s been a lot of discussion recently on how AI applications are evolving, but based on many of the announcements that Google (NASDAQ:GOOG, NASDAQ:GOOGL) made at their Cloud Next event in Las Vegas, it seems increasingly clear that hybrid is where many of these developments are headed.

To be clear, Google made an enormously and impressively broad range of announcements at Cloud Next and not a single press release specifically mentioned Hybrid AI. However, when you take a step back and analyze how several of the pieces fit together and look ahead to where the trends the company are driving appear to be going, the concept of GenAI-powered applications (and agents) that leverage a combination of the public cloud, enterprise private clouds and even smart devices - that is, Hybrid AI - appear inevitable.

A few highlights first. On the cloud infrastructure front, Google made several big debuts at Cloud Next, most of which focus on the increasing range of computer architecture options coming to customers of GCP. Most notably, the company took the wraps off their 7th generation TPU processor, codenamed Ironwood, its in-house designed alternative to GPUs and the first to be specifically designed for inferencing workloads. In addition to 10x improvements in raw performance versus previous generations, what’s impressive about the latest versions is the extent of high-speed chip-to-chip connectivity options that Google will be offering between them. Taking a page from the Nvidia (NVDA) NVLink book, Google’s latest AI Hypercomputer architecture lets up to 9,216 of these Gen 7 TPUs be interconnected into a single compute pod, providing plenty of bandwidth for even the largest of the new chain-of-thought based reasoning models starting to become available. In fact, Google claimed that maxing out a system could deliver up to 42.5 exaflops, more than 24x the computer power of today’s fastest supercomputer.

Another big theme from the Cloud Next keynote was around agents, including the tools to build them, to connect them to one another, and to more easily integrate them with a variety of LLMs. Building on the company’s previous Agentspace announcement - which allows enterprise employees to use Google’s multi-modal search capabilities across enterprise data and build their own agents in a low code/no code manner - Google also debuted a new Agent Development Kit for developers as part of its Vertex AI platform. Even more importantly, the company announced its Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol, which is an effort to standardize the means by which different agents can “talk” to each other and share information. A2A builds upon and is compatible with Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP) which was introduced last year and is quickly gaining traction in the AI world.

In fact, it’s Google’s strong MCP support across a range of products that it introduced here at Cloud Next that really led to the hybrid AI conclusions I made earlier. MCP offers a standardized way for models to connect to a variety of different data sources - instead of having to deal with proprietary APIs - and provides a standardized means by which models can expose the various functions they’re able to perform on these data sets. In the process, this means that MCP both solves some big challenges in creating AI-powered applications that can tap into local data resources and opens up a world of intriguing possibilities for creating distributed AI applications that can tap into data sources, other models and other computing infrastructure across different physical locations. It’s this capability that makes MCP so intriguing - and it’s likely a big reason support for the nascent standard is growing so rapidly.

Google made the potential impact of MCP much more real by announcing it is now also allowing organizations to bring Gemini models, Agentspace and other AI tools into their private cloud/on-prem datacenter environments via the Google Distributed Cloud in the third quarter of this year. This is a hugely important development because it means that companies building apps with Google Cloud-based tools can use them across many different environments.

So, for example, it would be possible for an organization to tap into the essentially unlimited resources of Google’s public cloud infrastructure to run certain functions with certain models and data sets stored there, while running other functions on different models that access data behind the firewall within their private cloud or datacenter environments. This solves the data gravity problem that many organizations have been struggling with as they start to think about tapping into the powerful capabilities of today’s most advanced LLMs, because it essentially allows them to have the best of both worlds. It gives them massive cloud-based compute with data stored in the public cloud and local compute with the large and often most valuable proprietary data sets that many organizations still keep (or may want to repatriate) back within their own environments. Plus, it’s even possible to extend the distributed nature of the computing environment to PCs and smartphones, particularly as the availability of devices with more powerful AI acceleration capabilities increases. While this last step won’t likely happen overnight, it will become a critical capability as companies look to reduce the electricity demands and costs of their AI applications down the road.

Speaking of on-device capabilities, Google also announced several enhancements to their Workspace productivity offering at this year’s Cloud Next. New AI-powered features include automation-focused Workflows, audio features in Docs and more. These build on many previous AI-powered functions that Google brought into Workspace earlier this year, including no-cost access to the most advanced version of the Gemini model, new data analysis functions in Sheets, document analysis and summarization across all the Workspace applications and more.

As with previous Cloud Next events, there were many more announcements that Google discussed across areas such as databases, code creation tools, the Firebase agent creation studio, Cloud WAN private network access, security improvements and much more. It’s a bit overwhelming to make sense of it all, to be honest, but it just shows how tremendously fast cloud-based offerings continue to expand, particularly with the integration of the even faster-moving AI foundation model developments. Ultimately, though, it’s clear that Google is using its long history of AI developments as well as the recent advancements it’s made with Gemini models and other AI tools as a clear differentiator for Google Cloud. In the process, they’re continuing to position themselves in a unique way not only for current applications but also for hybrid AI applications down the road.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

data.title: SPHY: Credit Turmoil Rattles Junk Bonds, Caution Still Warranted
data.description:
It has been a bloodbath among risk assets in the last week. Since President Trump's Liberation Day tariff announcement from the White House Rose Garden on the evening of Wednesday, April 2, the S&P 500 has fallen 15% from peak to trough.

Nothing has been unscathed. US stocks, global equities, Treasuries, commodities, and crypto have felt pain. The market is taking its medicine, as the president described it earlier this week aboard Air Force One.

One of the casualties of the sell-off has been US junk bonds. High-yield credit broke through key support earlier this month. I was neutral on the SPDR Portfolio High Yield Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:SPHY) in Q4 last year, and with a fast-moving bond market, a quick refresh is warranted. I reiterate a hold rating and will not possible areas on the chart to dabble at.

Recession Fears Bring About High Yield Credit Selling

According to the issuer, SPHY seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the price and yield performance of the ICE BofA US High Yield Index. It's a low-cost ETF that seeks to offer exposure to over a trillion dollars of USD-denominated high-yield debt. The index SPHY tracks includes publicly issued dollar high-yield bonds with a below investment grade rating, at least 18 months to final maturity at the time of issuance, at least one year to maturity, a fixed coupon, and a minimum amount outstanding of $250M.

SPHY has actually grown substantially since my December 2024 analysis. Total assets under management is now $7.8 billion, up from just $6.5 billion late last year. Its annual expense ratio is low at only 5 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 8.1%. SSGA Funds notes that the yield to maturity as of April 8, 2025, is 8.86%, and that is the rate prospective investors should expect to return, gross of fees and defaults, by holding the fund to its duration of 4.6 years.

Share-price momentum is rated highly by Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system, but that does not appear to capture the turmoil that has occurred in the past week, so investors should take caution with that ETF Grade in my view. Moreover, SPHY is an inherently risky product, given its high exposure to lower-quality corporate credit.

And while the liquidity grade is high, when markets turn volatile, I would not be surprised to see SPHY's bid/ask spread increase somewhat as traders scramble. When macro times are calm, the quant stats appear fine, but when correlations in markets go to 1, that's when a fund like SPHY can get sketchy-but that's also when opportunities present themselves.

Junk Bond YTM Moving Closer to 10%

For more color on the High Yield market, I'd call out that the junk bond spread has blown out to levels not seen since shortly after the Silicon Valley Bank crisis. Now at almost 500 basis points, investors are getting compensated for taking on low-grade credit risk. Given the current backdrop, however, we might have to test the 600 basis point mark on the chart below.

HY Spread Widens, 600bps Possible

Also consider that SPHY was a very strong play for years on end. The total return view asserts that while the latest drop is notable, it's not yet very significant compared to the bull run since October 2022.

SPHY Total Return Chart

From a holdings' perspective, a key reason why SPHY has been so poor lately is that it has a high weight to the Consumer Discretionary and Energy sectors. Those two spots have suffered mightily since Liberation Day. Consumer companies source goods from China, while WTI crude oil has plunged to fresh 50-month lows near $55. That's a bad combination for a fund like SPHY.

SPHY: High Discretionary & Energy Exposure

The Technical Take

Amid turmoil in High Yield credit, there is some hope that technical levels could support a bounce. Notice in the chart below that SPHY has pulled back to an uptrend support line from the October 2022 low. Given the brutal RSI momentum reading of 16-the lowest since the depths of COVID-I suspect the fund will go lower from here. I'd eye at least the $21.80 mark from October 2023. That should correspond closer to the 600 basis point High Yield spread level mentioned earlier.

Now with a bearish death cross in play, and a 200-day moving average that is poised to turn south, the bears control the primary trend. Upgrading the fund to a buy today would just be based on hope. History reveals that such downturns lead to lower prices and higher spreads. Still, with a YTM near 9%, the risk/reward appears closer to balanced, given the broad environment. For perspective, the March 2020 spike low was $20.29--about 10% lower from the current level.

SPHY: Shares Plunge, Near-Term Support at the October 2022 Low

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on SPHY. The YTM soared to 9%, but I think it could reach double digits with a 600bps junk bond spread in the offing. At which time, the ETF would be a better risk/reward play, and I'd likely upgrade to buy.

data.title: Mawer Investment Management Q1 2025 Quarterly Update
data.description:
Market Overview

The first quarter of 2025 saw notable shifts in global economic momentum. Escalating tariff threats and trade tensions—which have compounded even further since the end of the quarter—cloud the economic outlook globally while intensifying inflationary pressures. This creates a challenging environment for central banks tasked with managing inflation while countering slowing growth.

Germany’s fiscal paradigm shift has generated a positive, near-term market response, while the economic response may take longer. As a result, European equities outperformed this quarter led by long-term defense and infrastructure commitments and relatively lower valuations compared to U.S. equities. Meanwhile, China’s near-term growth has been buoyed by rising private sector confidence and advancements in artificial intelligence, such as contributions from DEEPSEEK, a new Chinese AI Large Language Model.

Canadian equities outperformed U.S. equities this quarter, largely due to the continued surge in gold prices, which significantly benefited Canada’s mining sector. The rise in gold was driven by global economic uncertainty, increased central bank demand for diversification away from U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) holdings, and limited supply growth.

Despite this positive momentum, Canada faces challenges from U.S. tariffs and political uncertainty during the federal election campaign. Business confidence has been dampened as trade tensions dominate the political discourse. Ongoing interest rate cuts by the Bank of Canada were meant to counteract the economic softness but have yet to fully restore confidence.

The ongoing trade disputes underscore the importance of maintaining a diversified portfolio that balances growth opportunities with risk management, particularly as traditional sector leaders face headwinds and new market dynamics emerge.

How Did We Do?

Performance has been presented for the O-Series Mawer Mutual Funds in Canadian dollars and calculated gross of management fees and net of operating expenses for the 3-month period ending March 31, 2025.

Perhaps surprisingly given the backdrop, the first quarter of 2025 delivered positive returns for Mawer Balanced Fund investors. Relative performance versus the benchmark was strong, driven primarily by components that had exhibited more difficult relative returns in 2024. Our U.S. equity strategy benefited from our focus on high-quality, stable business models while avoiding some high-flying technology and consumer stocks such as Nvidia (NVDA), Tesla (TSLA), and Apple (AAPL). Our global small cap equity strategy exceeded expectations this quarter due to its underweight exposure to the U.S. and greater emphasis on international markets where valuations remain more favorable.

Fixed income assets also played their traditional role effectively in our balanced strategies. Canadian bonds provided income and stability while partially hedging against equity market volatility. Similarly, those invested in our global credit opportunities strategy saw positive returns thanks to falling government bond yields and coupon income, though widening credit spreads offset some gains.

The spot price of gold continued to rise throughout the quarter driven by increasing demand for the precious metal and limited supply growth. One of the biggest factors that detracted from relative performance in Canada was a lack of exposure to gold mining stocks.

Balanced and Fixed Income: Chart A: Q1 2025, Series O, Gross of fees

Some of our stronger performing international equity holdings this quarter were defense-related: Germany’s Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) more-than-doubled during the quarter, while France’s Thales (OTCPK:THLEF), UK’s BAE (OTCPK:BAESF), and Italy’s Leonardo (OTCPK:FINMF) also experienced stellar returns. A rapidly evolving geopolitical landscape and rising defense spending in Europe have significantly increased demand expectations. Additionally, all four companies stand to benefit from the critical importance of their products, and decades of consolidation and underinvestment in areas where they hold key competitive advantages.

A volatile backdrop proved beneficial for several of our exchange businesses in driving greater interest in overall hedging activity and higher trading volumes. Deutsche Boerse has been a beneficiary of greater interest rate volatility and volumes on its platforms, as have TMX Group (OTCPK:TMXXF) and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), both of which have benefited from higher volumes on their energy platforms.

There were also strong performing businesses in the financials sector. In the insurance brokerage industry, holdings such as Arthur J. Gallagher (AJG), Marsh & McLennan (MMC), and AON, delivered steady organic growth and margin improvements during the past quarter. Canadian-based Intact Financial (OTCPK:IFCZF) showed impressive earnings growth, driven by strong underwriting performance in addition to benefitting from favorable market conditions which led to a 5% increase in premiums.

Another business that performed well this quarter was AltaGas (OTCPK:ATGFF), a utility operator and midstream company. AltaGas continued to generate strong returns across its two primary business lines. Notably, its midstream pipeline operations in Canada appear well-positioned to capitalize on opportunities arising from the ongoing trade tensions with the U.S.

However, some of our semiconductor holdings took a breather this quarter after a brilliant few years prior. Leading manufacturer TSMC (TSM) and single wafer atomic deposition provider ASM International (ASML) suffered in part due to concerns about the sustainability of AI growth and how tariffs may disrupt the global supply chain. CGI was also affected by news south of the border as negative sentiment from potential Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) cost cutting measures weighed on many consulting businesses. CGI has relatively modest exposure to the U.S. government and most of its contracts are for software that integrates and manages critical functions like procurement and finance.

Some financial holdings, including Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS), Royal Bank, and Brookfield Corporation (BN), also saw a pause after a strong 2024. In contrast, TD Bank (TD) performed well this quarter, driven by the accelerated transition of CEO Raymond Chun and significant board changes which were positively received by the market. However, we remain cautious on the broader banking sector due to headwinds from slowing loan growth and rising credit losses amid potential economic challenges in Canada.

Equities: Chart B: Q1 2025, Series O, Gross of fees

Looking Ahead

The global economic and geopolitical backdrop continues to evolve rapidly, marked by significant transitions and heightened uncertainty. The dramatic escalation in trade barriers, Germany’s C1 trillion commitment to defense and infrastructure spending, and the steady stream of executive orders from the Trump administration highlight the scale of these changes. However, this uncertainty evokes a sense of hesitation across consumers, central banks, and companies alike—akin to a four-way traffic stop where all parties wait for others to act.

Canada’s economic outlook remains mixed as the Bank of Canada (BoC) faces mounting challenges. While interest rate cuts were made to support household spending and business investment, weak consumer and business confidence is evident in higher savings rates, reduced spending, and cautious investment plans. Inflation expectations are rising, with many businesses planning to pass on price increases, creating a dilemma for the BoC. The BoC may continue to ease modestly but may pause if inflation expectations rise more than anticipated. Adding to this uncertainty is the upcoming federal election on April 28, which comes amidst escalating trade tensions with the U.S., threats to Canadian sovereignty by President Trump, and competing visions from party leaders. The election’s outcome could significantly reshape fiscal policy and economic priorities in Canada.

At times like these, temperament matters as much as analysis. The temptation to react impulsively—to slam on the brakes or to accelerate too quickly—can be costly. While transitions create uncertainty, they also generate opportunity for those who remain clear-eyed.

The road ahead will have its twists, but our approach remains the same: prioritize resilience over reaction and discipline over distraction. Our North Star: a focus on attractively valued, well-run businesses that can withstand turbulence by, quite simply, selling a good or a service their clients value at a price that more-than-covers the cost of capital by virtue of a competitive advantage, thereby creating wealth.

Just like at a busy intersection, apply the right balance of focus, patience, and decisiveness.

data.title: Billionaire Investor Warns Of 'Economic Nuclear Winter' -- Our Picks
data.description:
Billionaire investor Bill Ackman just sounded the alarm and stated that we are headed towards a "self-induced economic nuclear winter" unless President Trump reverses course on his tariff plans.

And before you discredit him as having "Trump Derangement Syndrome" or being a poor investor due to his few big losses, consider that he has actually been a strong Trump supporter, helping him win in the election, and despite having suffered some high-profile losses (such as Valeant), he still has one of the best track records in the world, having massively outperformed the rest of the market (SPY), resulting in him becoming a self-made billionaire.

I would add that he is not shorting the market, he has no margin or other liquidity issues, and most of his capital is permanent, so he is not worried about redemptions.

His only conflict, if it even is one, is that he is invested in the market and wants the American and global economies to do well in order to earn a good return on his investments.

So why is he sounding the alarm?

I have spent a lot of time reading his many tweets, responses, and retweets of other commentators.

My take is that he is very concerned about the broader economy because of the following 10 reasons:

  1. Tariffs could greatly increase the price of goods for consumers, reducing confidence and consumption.
  2. It could also increase the cost of goods for American businesses, reducing their profit margins and making them less competitive on the global stage.
  3. It will lead to retaliation from major trading partners, making it harder for American companies to sell their products abroad. China has already issued major tariffs on American companies, putting them at a major disadvantage in accessing its massive market.
  4. It will also lead to indirect economic pain from foreigners boycotting American products. Tesla (TSLA) is a prime example of that, as its foreign sales are down significantly, but other American products could also be impacted going forward.
  5. It will also lead to lower international tourism to the US. For example, flight bookings from Canada to the US are down 70%.
  6. It hurts the US Dollar by lowering the demand for it. It has gone down sharply in recent days, making it more expensive for Americans to import goods and travel abroad.
  7. It hurts business confidence because there is just too much uncertainty, causing companies to delay major investments and future hiring.
  8. The uncertainty also leads to a higher risk premium, increasing the cost of equity for companies. It makes it harder for public companies to raise capital, but even more so for smaller private companies.
  9. The US tariffs are now pushing foreign countries to create new economic relations that replace the US. As an example, China will seek to buy more soybeans from Brazil and tech from Japan and South Korea. In fact, China, Japan, and South Korea just held their first major economic dialogue in five years to coordinate a plan in response to the tariffs.
  10. Finally, it is not clear that it will revive domestic manufacturing. Even with major tariffs in place, it is still generally cheaper to produce in countries like Vietnam than in the US. Moreover, few companies will be ready to green-light major investments into building new manufacturing plants because there is just too much uncertainty. Trump has already shown with Canada and Mexico that he can impose major tariffs and talk tough, just to remove them a week later. Companies will not go on a 5-year journey to build a new plant if they think that tariffs could go away in a few months, or at the latest, when the next administration comes into power.

He has publicly called for President Trump to call a 90-day time-out to negotiate privately with other countries and protect the economy.

He says that otherwise, we will "destroy confidence in our country as a trading partner, as a place to do business, and as a market to invest capital."

In his most popular tweet, he adds that:

If, on the other hand, on April 9th, we launch an economic nuclear war on every country in the world, business investment will grind to a halt, consumers will close their wallets and pocketbooks, and we will severely damage our reputation with the rest of the world that will take years and potentially decades to rehabilitate.

What CEO and what board of directors will be comfortable making large, long-term, economic commitments in our country in the middle of an economic nuclear war? I don’t know of one who will do so.

When markets crash, new investment stops, consumers stop spending money, and businesses have no choice but to curtail investment and fire workers.

And it is not just the big companies that will suffer. Small- and medium-sized businesses and entrepreneurs will experience much greater pain. Almost no business can pass through an overnight massive increase in costs to their customers. And that’s true even if they have no debt, and, unfortunately, there is a massive amount of leverage in the system.

Business is a confidence game. The president is losing the confidence of business leaders around the globe. The consequences for our country and the millions of our citizens who have supported the president, in particular low-income consumers who are already under a huge amount of economic stress, are going to be severely negative. This is not what we voted for.

The President has an opportunity on Monday to call a time-out and have the time to execute on fixing an unfair tariff system.

Alternatively, we are heading for a self-induced, economic nuclear winter, and we should start hunkering down.

May cooler heads prevail. [emphasis added]

Ouch!

Some still hope that this is just a negotiation strategy to get other countries to remove trade barriers, but I am not so sure. He has been very consistent about this particular topic for decades, and he seems very serious if you listen to his recent discussions with the press.

The unfortunate reality is that the damage is already done.

Even if President Trump now pulls out and claims victory for winning some concessions, I expect still economic damage from this in the near term as this disruption will have led to significant uncertainty, hurt confidence, damaged international relationships, and will potentially lead to less investment going forward.

So, in my mind, the question is not if we are going into a recession. The question is how damaging it will be.

How should you invest in such a challenging environment?

Ideally, you would invest in market sectors that:

  • are recession-resistant,
  • not impacted by tariffs,
  • are not heavily leveraged,
  • can pass inflation on to clients,
  • and are priced at low valuations to offer margin of safety.

With that in mind, one of my favorite sectors of the market is that of real estate investment trusts, or REITs in short.

Most of them own highly resilient properties that our society cannot live without. Think of things like cell towers, affordable housing, farmland, hospitals, data centers, or grocery stores.

The demand for these properties is resilient to recessions, and they also enjoy long-term leases that result in highly consistent and predictable rental income even through recessions.

As a result, REITs (VNQ) have been far more stable than other stocks during most recessions:

They are also not heavily impacted by tariffs in most cases.

Major global companies like Nike (NKE) and Apple (AAPL) are heavily impacted, but REITs operate domestic businesses and do not trade with foreign countries.

Whether China imposes major tariffs or not, people will still use their phones to surf the web in the US, requiring cell towers. They will also need a roof over their heads, requiring apartments. They will need to buy, requiring grocery stores. Etc.

REITs are also in a strong financial position to withstand a crisis. Their leverage is at an all-time low, with debt-to-asset values at just 35%, and debt maturities are long and well-staggered.

Finally, and perhaps most importantly, REIT valuations are at the lowest level in a decade, with most REITs trading at low multiples of their cash flows and steep discounts relative to the private market value of their properties.

This should provide a margin of safety going forward, and it may explain why REITs have been outperforming the rest of the market so far this year:

Finally, the silver lining in all of this is that if we go into a recession, interest rates are likely to drop to much lower levels, and this should benefit REITs.

The 10-year Treasury has already dropped from nearly 5% earlier this year to just 4% today, which should allow REITs to refinance their debt at lower rates.

The tariffs will also increase the cost of construction, increase the replacement value of existing properties, reduce future supply, and accelerate rent growth in certain property sectors.

But what are the best REITs to buy today?

There are lots of opportunities right now, and it is difficult to pick just one.

There are many that we are accumulating at these levels at High-Yield Landlord.

One that we like a lot is called Alexandria Real Estate (ARE). It is the leader in the life science property sector. These healthcare properties are recession-resistant and should not be heavily affected by the trade wars because pharmaceuticals have been exempted from the tariffs.

Moreover, Alexandria has 8-year long leases on average to protect through the downcycle, and low leverage with a strong BBB+ investment grade rating.

Its valuation is at a decade low because its sector is today oversupplied, but rent should already accelerate in 2026 as new construction activity has dropped to historically low levels and will only drop further as a result of the tariffs.

To return to its historical average valuation multiple, the stock would need to more than double from here, and while we wait, we earn a near 7% dividend yield.

Current FFO Multiple Historic FFO Multiple
Alexandria Real Estate 8x FFO 20-25x

Another REIT that we like at these levels is BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / OTCPK:BSRTF).

I think that we can all agree that affordable apartments that rent for $1,500 per month in rapidly growing Texan markets will remain in high demand, regardless of what happens to this trade war or the broader economy.

These properties have suffered stagnating rents in recent years due to oversupply, but the new supply has already dropped to a decade-low, and our prediction is that it will drop even more as construction costs will go way up as a result of the tariffs.

This should lead to a strong acceleration in rent growth over the coming years, and yet, the REIT is trading at a 35% discount today compared to our estimate of the fair market value of its properties. The upside potential is about 50%, and we earn a 4% monthly tax-advantaged dividend yield while we wait (classified as return of capital).

The yield may not seem that high, but this is only because the REIT retains nearly half of its cash flow to buy back shares.

Closing Note

The outlook is extremely uncertain right now, and we could face major pain before the economy recovers.

At High-Yield Landlord, we are playing defense by positioning our portfolio to own mostly essential infrastructure that's today discounted, offering high upside, steady cash flow, and inflation protection.
